critique.summary: The article explains the differences between Linux's compressed swap technologies zswap and zram, recommending zswap for most use cases due to its tighter integration with the kernel's memory management and automatic tiering. It highlights zram's limitations, such as lack of automatic eviction and potential performance issues, making it suitable mainly for embedded or diskless systems with specific constraints.
mainPoints: zswap acts as a compression layer in front of disk swap, automatically evicting cold pages to disk and integrating with the kernel's memory management.
zram creates a compressed block device in RAM with a fixed capacity and no automatic eviction, which can lead to LRU inversion and system hangs under pressure.
Using zram alongside disk swap can degrade performance by filling fast RAM with cold pages and pushing active pages to slow disk.
zswap handles incompressible data better by rejecting it and sending it directly to disk, while zram compresses all data regardless of compressibility.
zswap reduces SSD wear by absorbing frequent page-out/page-in operations in RAM, whereas zram can increase disk I/O by forcing more page cache eviction.
Fedora uses a zram-only approach with systemd-oomd to avoid disk swap for security and complexity reasons, but this setup requires careful management to avoid system hangs.
Future kernel development favors zswap as the single compressed swap implementation, potentially with backing-store-less operation, reducing the need for zram.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a detailed, technically accurate explanation of zswap and zram based on kernel internals and real-world experience, including references to kernel versions, code snippets, and production use cases. The claims about zswap's advantages in integration, automatic tiering, and handling of memory pressure are consistent with Linux kernel documentation and expert commentary. The discussion of zram's limitations, such as lack of automatic eviction and LRU inversion, aligns with known issues reported by kernel developers and users. The article also correctly notes Fedora's rationale for using zram-only setups and the ongoing kernel development trends favoring zswap. Overall, the information is well-supported and credible.
considerations: The article focuses primarily on Linux kernel memory management and may not cover all use cases or hardware configurations where zram could be beneficial.
Performance and behavior can vary depending on workload characteristics, kernel versions, and system tuning, so empirical testing is recommended for specific environments.
Security considerations around swap data on persistent storage versus RAM are important but only briefly touched on.
The article assumes familiarity with Linux kernel internals and may be challenging for less technical readers.
The impact of newer kernel features or alternative compressed swap solutions beyond zswap and zram is not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the use and configuration of zswap and zram for swap management in Linux systems. Users debate the assumptions about SSD suitability for swap, ease of setup, and potential performance implications, with calls for more data and practical advice.

comments.author: patrakov
comments.text:
User here, who also acts as a Level 2 support for storage.

The article contains some solid logic plus an assumption that I disagree with.

Solid logic: you should prefer zswap if you have a device that can be used for swap.

Solid logic: zram + other swap = bad due to LRU inversion (zram becomes a dead weight in memory).

Advice that matches my observations: zram works best when paired with a user-space OOM killer.

Bold assumption: everybody who has an SSD has a device that can be used for swap.

The assumption is simply false, and not due to the "SSD wear" argument. Many consumer SSDs, especially DRAMless ones (e.g., Apacer AS350 1TB, but also seen on Crucial SSDs), under synchronous writes, will regularly produce latency spikes of 10 seconds or more, due to the way they need to manage their cells. This is much worse than any HDD. If a DRAMless consumer SSD is all that you have, better use zram.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced critique based on real-world hardware behavior, challenging a common assumption with specific examples, making it highly credible and insightful.

comments.author: guenthert
comments.text: So much polemic and no numbers?  If it is a performance issue, show me the numbers!
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the need for empirical data to support performance claims, encouraging a more rigorous approach to the discussion.

comments.author: prussian
comments.text:
With zram, I can just use zram-generator[0] and it does everything for me and I don't even need to set anything up, other than installing the systemd generator, which on some distros, it's installed by default. Is there anything equivalent for zswap? Otherwise, I'm not surprised most people are just using zram, even if sub-optimal.

[0]: https://crates.io/crates/zram-generator
comments.analysis: This comment points out practical usability differences between zram and zswap, which is relevant for adoption and user experience considerations.

comments.author: CoolGuySteve
comments.text:
Would be nice if zswap could be configured to have no backing cache so it could completely replace zram. Having two slightly different systems is weird.

There's not really any difference between swap on disk being full and swap in ram being full, either way something needs to get OOM killed.

Simplifying the configuration would probably also make it easier to enable by default in most distros. It's kind of backwards that the most common Linux distros other than ChromeOS are behind Mac and Windows in this regard.
comments.analysis: The comment suggests improvements to zswap to unify swap solutions and simplify configuration, reflecting a forward-looking and practical perspective.

comments.author: nephanth
comments.text:
I used to put swap on zram when my laptop had one of those early ssds, that people would tell you not to put swap on for fear of wearing them out

Setup was tedious
comments.analysis: This personal anecdote adds context about past user experience with early SSDs and zram, though it is less detailed and more subjective.

comments.author: Mashimo
comments.text: Is this advice also applicable to Desktops installations?
comments.analysis: A straightforward question seeking clarification on the applicability of the advice, indicating engagement but no direct contribution to accuracy.

comments.author: adgjlsfhk1
comments.text: can you make a follow-up here for the best way to setup swap to support full disk encryption+hybernation?
comments.analysis: This comment requests additional information on a specific use case, showing interest but not adding substantive analysis.

comments.author: jitl
comments.text: thank goodness Kubernetes got support for swap; zswap has been a great boon for one of my workloads
comments.analysis: A brief endorsement of zswap's utility in a Kubernetes context, which is positive but lacks detailed explanation or evidence.
